unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit, your child learns about their five senses — seeing, hearing, smelling, tasting and touching — and the body parts we use for each one. They learn describing words (soft, loud, sweet, cold) and start to compare things (a tree is tall, a building is taller). Most of all, they explore the world around them and learn to be curious and thankful for all the wonderful things they can sense.
unit.learningPath:
Begin with the guided teacher launch and learn a few words through pictures and play.
Listen to the Unit story and join in with repeated language.
Complete six language, speaking, early-writing and practical activities.
Meet your teacher three times each week for two weeks.
Finish the ten-question checkpoint and tell an adult what you can do.

visual.alt: Child-friendly illustration for My Five Senses

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To use your eyes to look at something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I can see the moon tonight.
practiceSentences: I see my friends at school.
What can you see outside?
Musa, come and see this bird!
My eyes help me see clearly.
We see bright stars at night.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: s - e - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say see slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To use your ears to know a sound.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I can hear the birds singing.
practiceSentences: I hear the adhan from the mosque.
Can you hear that sound?
Samira, listen and hear the rain!
My ears help me hear well.
We hear our teacher's kind voice.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: h - e - a - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say hear slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To use your nose to find out what something is like.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: s - m - e - l - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say smell slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To use your tongue to know if food is sweet or salty.

practiceSentences: I taste the honey on my bread.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: t - a - s - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say taste slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To use your hands or skin to feel something.

practiceSentences: I touch the cold window with my hand.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: t - o - u - c - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say touch slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The two parts of your face that help you see.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My eyes help me read my book.
practiceSentences: My eyes are dark brown.
Can you close your eyes?

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: e - y - e - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say eyes slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The two parts on your head that help you hear.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My ears hear the birds singing.
practiceSentences: My ears are small and round.
Are your ears cold today?
Yusuf, cover your ears!

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: e - a - r - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say ears slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The part of your face that helps you smell.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My nose smells the fresh bread.
practiceSentences: My nose is cold in winter.
Can you wrinkle your nose?

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: n - o - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say nose slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The soft pink part inside your mouth that tastes food.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My tongue tastes the sweet honey.

Can you see your tongue?

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The part of your face you use to eat and talk.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The two parts at the end of your arms.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say sweeter slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

practiceSentences: My hands are colder than my face.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say colder slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say juicier slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name the five senses: see, hear, smell, taste, touch.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Point to and name the body parts we use: eyes, ears, nose, tongue, hands.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say which sense goes with which body part (“I see with my eyes”).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use describing words: soft, hard, loud, quiet, sweet, cold.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Compare two things (tall / taller; sweet / sweeter).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Join in the “Two Little Eyes” poem and “The Music Man” song.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

readings.title: Amal at the Market
readings.theme: the senses, body parts, descriptions and comparison
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript:
One sunny morning, Amal went to the market with her grandmother, Selma.

The market was big and busy. “Come, Amal,” said Selma. “Use your five senses today!”

First, Amal used her eyes to see. She saw red tomatoes and yellow bananas. She saw green mangoes in a big basket. “I can see so many colours!” said Amal.

Next, Amal used her ears to hear. She heard a man calling, “Fresh fish! Fresh fish!” She heard goats and a baby crying. “It is very loud here!” she said.

Then Amal used her nose to smell. She smelled warm bread. She smelled sweet flowers. She smelled hot spices from a big pot. “Mmm! This smells so good!” said Amal.

A kind man named Omar gave Amal a piece of ripe mango. She used her tongue to taste it. “It is sweet and juicy!” she said. “Thank you, Omar!”

Last, Amal used her hands to touch. She touched a soft cloth. It felt soft. She touched a smooth stone. It felt hard and cold. “I like to touch things,” said Amal.

“Look, Grandmother!” said Amal. “This melon is big. But that melon is bigger!”

Selma smiled. “Well done, Amal. You are using all your senses.”

On the way home, Amal held her grandmother's hand. “I saw pretty colours. I heard loud sounds. I smelled sweet bread. I tasted juicy mango. And I touched soft cloth,” she said.

“Alhamdulillah,” said Selma. “We must be thankful for our wonderful senses.”

“Yes,” said Amal happily. “Thank you, Allah, for my eyes, my ears, my nose, my tongue, and my hands!”

readings.title: Talk about My Five Senses
readings.theme: the senses, body parts, descriptions and comparison
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript: Look, point and talk. I can see with my eyes. I can hear with my ears. It is ___. This is ___ than that. These are my ___. Which sense do I use? An adult reads each line while the learner points, repeats and acts.

readings.title: My Five Senses rhyme
readings.theme: the senses, body parts, descriptions and comparison
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript:
“Two Little Eyes” — say it together
Two little eyes to see all around. / Two little ears to hear each sound. / One little nose to smell what's sweet. / One little mouth that likes to eat.
Point to your eyes, ears, nose and mouth as you say each line. Then ask: which sense is NOT in the poem? (Touch! Wiggle your fingers.)
“The Music Man” — sing and pretend to play
I am the music man. I come from down your way. And I can play. What can you play? / I can play the piano, the piano, the piano. I can play the piano, pia-piano.
Pretend to play each instrument as you sing — piano, drum, violin. This helps your child listen for different sounds.

comprehension.question: Who took Amal to the busy market?
comprehension.explanation: The story begins with Amal going to the market with her grandmother, Selma.

comprehension.question: Which sense did Amal use for the piece of ripe mango?
comprehension.explanation: Amal used her tongue to taste the mango and said it was sweet and juicy.

comprehension.question: What was the man calling out in the market?
comprehension.explanation: Amal used her ears and heard a man calling, 'Fresh fish! Fresh fish!'

comprehension.question: The stone felt hard and cold. How did the cloth feel?
comprehension.explanation: Amal touched a soft cloth and then a smooth stone that felt hard and cold.

comprehension.question: Which sense did Amal use first at the market?
comprehension.explanation: 'First, Amal used her eyes to see' the red tomatoes and yellow bananas.

comprehension.question: Why did Selma say 'Alhamdulillah' at the end?
comprehension.correctAnswer: To give thanks for their wonderful senses
comprehension.explanation: Selma says they must be thankful for their wonderful senses.

comprehension.question: Which talk line names the part you hear with?
comprehension.correctAnswer: I can hear with my ears.
comprehension.explanation: The second talk line is 'I can hear with my ears.'

comprehension.question: Say 'This is ___ than that' about two melons.
comprehension.correctAnswer: This melon is bigger than that melon.
comprehension.explanation: 'This is ___ than that' is the talk line for comparing two things.

comprehension.question: Which talk line do you say while pointing to your eyes?
comprehension.correctAnswer: I can see with my eyes.
comprehension.explanation: The first talk line is 'I can see with my eyes.'

comprehension.question: Hold up your hands and say, 'These are my ___.'
comprehension.explanation: 'These are my ___' is the talk line for naming your body parts.

comprehension.question: Touch something and finish this line: 'It is ___.'
comprehension.correctAnswer: It is soft. (any true describing word)
comprehension.explanation: 'It is ___' is the talk line for describing what you touch.

comprehension.question: One talk line is a question. Say that question.
comprehension.explanation: 'Which sense do I use?' is the only question in the talk lines.

grammar.title: Eyes are for seeing
grammar.explanation: Point to your eyes and say the whole sentence. Your eyes are the sense that helps you see.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: I can see with my eyes.
grammar.commonMistake: 'My eyes' sometimes becomes 'my eye'. You have two, so say 'eyes' with a /z/ sound at the end.
grammar.memoryTip: Cover your eyes, then open them wide as you say the sentence.
grammar.practice: Say the sentence, then name three things you can see: a window, a book, a friend.

grammar.title: Ears are for hearing
grammar.explanation: Cup your ears with your hands and listen carefully. Then say the whole sentence out loud.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: I can hear with my ears.
grammar.commonMistake: 'Hear' and 'here' sound the same. Show the child that h-EAR has the word EAR hiding inside it.
grammar.memoryTip: The word 'hear' has 'ear' inside it. Look for it!
grammar.practice: Close your eyes, listen, then name three sounds: a bird, a door, a car.

grammar.title: Describe how something feels
grammar.explanation: Touch something safely, then describe it. Choose a feeling word like soft, rough or cold.
grammar.commonMistake: Children may name the object instead of describing it. Ask 'How does it feel?' to get a describing word.
grammar.memoryTip: Feel it with your fingertips first, then choose your word.
grammar.practice: Touch three things and say: It is soft. It is rough. It is cold.

grammar.title: Compare two things
grammar.explanation: Hold one thing in each hand and compare them. Add -er to your describing word.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children say 'This is more soft than that.' Short words simply take -er: 'softer'.
grammar.memoryTip: One thing in each hand, and the -er word wins.
grammar.practice: Compare three pairs: This is softer than that. This is louder than that. This is sweeter than that.

grammar.title: Use 'these' for more than one
grammar.explanation: When you have two or more things, use 'these'. Point to both as you speak.
grammar.commonMistake: 'This is my hands' mixes one and many. Two or more take 'These are'.
grammar.memoryTip: One thing: this is. Many things: these are. Wiggle all your fingers for 'these'.
grammar.practice: Point and say three: These are my eyes. These are my ears. These are my hands.

grammar.title: Ask which sense you are using
grammar.explanation: Ask this question, then work out the answer together: eyes, ears, nose, tongue or hands.
grammar.commonMistake: Children may answer with the object instead of the sense. Ask again: which body part is doing the work?
grammar.memoryTip: Count the five senses on your five fingers before you answer.
grammar.practice: Ask and answer three times: Smelling bread, my nose. Tasting dates, my tongue. Hearing rain, my ears.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 - Listen and point
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Point to the body part an adult names, then say the sentence back. Say: 'I can see with my eyes.' 'I can smell with my nose.' 'These are my eyes.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern I can see with my eyes. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 - Repeat the model
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Close your eyes, listen, then tell your partner one sound you heard. Say: 'I can hear with my ears.' 'I can hear a bird.' 'I can hear the adhan.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern I can hear with my ears. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 - Ask and answer
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Feel something hidden in a bag and describe it. Say: 'It is soft.' 'It is hard and cold.' 'What is it?'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern It is ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 - Picture talk
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Hold two things and compare them for your partner. Say: 'This is softer than that.' 'This is bigger than that.' 'This one is louder.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern This is ___ than that. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 - Role-play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Play a body-part game and point as you speak. Say: 'These are my hands.' 'These are my ears.' 'This is my tongue.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern These are my ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 - My confident turn
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Ask the class your own sense riddle. Say: 'I taste honey. Which sense do I use?' 'I use my tongue.' 'Now try my next one.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern Which sense do I use? Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

writing.title: Writing 1 - Draw and tell
writing.promptAndInstructions: Draw your face with eyes, ears, a nose and a mouth. Tell an adult what each part helps you do.
writing.modelText: I can see with my eyes.
writing.sentenceStarter: I can see with my eyes.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 2 - Trace
writing.promptAndInstructions: Trace the words 'ears' and 'eyes'. Touch that body part after each word, then trace them both once more.
writing.modelText: I can hear with my ears.
writing.sentenceStarter: I can hear with my ears.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 3 - Match and copy
writing.promptAndInstructions: Match the words soft, loud and sweet to the right pictures. Then copy the word that describes a cat's fur.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 4 - Label
writing.promptAndInstructions: Add two labels to your face drawing: 'nose' and 'mouth'. Point to each label and say what that part does.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 5 - Complete a pattern
writing.promptAndInstructions: Copy and finish twice: 'These are my ___.' Use hands and ears. Remember the letter s at the end of each word.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 6 - Create one sentence
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose a sense picture. Write one sentence: 'I use my ___.' Then ask a friend, 'Which sense do I use?'
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

activities.title: Activity 1
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw a line from each body part to its job: eyes to see, ears to hear, nose to smell, tongue to taste. Say each pair aloud.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 2
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw something you love to look at, such as the night sky. Then say: 'I can see a ___. It is beautiful.'
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 3
activities.instructionsAndItems: Colour the things you can smell: bread, flowers, soap and mint tea. Then tell an adult which smell you like best.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 4
activities.instructionsAndItems: Play a listening game. Close your eyes while an adult claps, taps, pours water and knocks. Guess and name each sound.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 5
activities.instructionsAndItems: Touch four things and say how each one feels: soft, hard, rough or smooth. Then sort them into two piles.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 6
activities.instructionsAndItems: Make a tasting tray with dates, bread and lemon. Taste each one and tell your family: 'It is sweet.' 'It is sour.'
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

assignments.title: My My Five Senses show-and-tell page
assignments.instructions: Make a five senses collection. 1) With a grown-up, find safe things at home that are soft, loud, sweet, cold and round. 2) Draw or stick them onto one page. 3) Label two of them with traced describing words. 4) Record yourself saying: I can see with my eyes. I can hear with my ears. It is soft. These are my hands. 5) Hold up two things and compare them: This is colder than that.
assignments.submissionType: Drawing or object + labels + short audio/video + reflection

quizzes.question: Which body part do you use to see?
quizzes.explanation: We see with our eyes; ears are for hearing, the nose is for smelling and hands are for touching.

quizzes.question: Which body part do you use to hear?
quizzes.explanation: We hear with our ears; eyes are for seeing, the tongue is for tasting and feet are for walking.

quizzes.question: Which body part do you use to smell a flower?
quizzes.explanation: We smell with our nose; hands touch, ears hear and eyes see.

quizzes.question: Which sense do you use with your tongue?
quizzes.explanation: The tongue tastes food; seeing, hearing and smelling use the eyes, ears and nose.

quizzes.question: Which word names one of your five senses?
quizzes.explanation: Touch is a sense; jump is an action, a pencil is a thing and Monday is a day.

quizzes.question: Aisha holds a piece of ice. How does it feel?
quizzes.explanation: Ice feels cold when you touch it; loud is a sound, bright is a sight and sweet is a taste.

quizzes.question: Which word tells how honey tastes?
quizzes.explanation: Honey tastes sweet; noisy is for sounds, shiny is for looks and rough is for touch.

quizzes.question: Which of these can you hear?
quizzes.options: a soft blanket | a red flower | a sweet date | a bell ringing
quizzes.explanation: A ringing bell makes a sound; the flower is for looking, the blanket for touching and the date for tasting.

quizzes.question: Which sentence about our senses is true?
quizzes.options: I can see with my eyes. | I can hear with my nose. | I can see with my ears. | I can smell with my hands.
quizzes.correctAnswer: I can see with my eyes.
quizzes.explanation: Eyes are for seeing; ears hear, the nose smells and hands touch, so the other sentences are mixed up.

quizzes.question: Which sentence compares two things?
quizzes.options: This box is heavy. | This box is heavier than that box. | The box is brown. | I can see the box.
quizzes.correctAnswer: This box is heavier than that box.
quizzes.explanation: The word than compares two things; the other sentences only describe one box.

liveSessions.title: Meet the words
liveSessions.beforeSession: Bring crayons and one real object connected to the Unit.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min hello song; 7 min model and repeat; 8 min game or story; 7 min guided practice; 3 min celebrate and preview
liveSessions.afterSession: Show one word, action, drawing or sentence from My Five Senses to an adult.

teacherNotes.note: Use 15-20 minute independent blocks inside the lesson. Read every instruction aloud, model before expecting a response, and accept pointing, acting, drawing, tracing or speaking as valid evidence.
A teacher or caregiver remains present whenever the young learner uses recording or AI tutor features. The adult controls the device and protects the child's personal information.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name the five senses: see, hear, smell, taste, touch.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can point to and name the body parts we use: eyes, ears, nose, tongue, hands.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say which sense goes with which body part (“I see with my eyes”).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use describing words: soft, hard, loud, quiet, sweet, cold.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can compare two things (tall / taller; sweet / sweeter).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can join in the “Two Little Eyes” poem and “The Music Man” song.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
